message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>
we have so many docs and mds and material in this repo. what I want from you is to analyze it all and rethink a proper structured way to do this. and remove non important stuff.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by scouting the repo to understand what docs and material exist, then orchestrate a thorough analysis. Let me map out the documentation landscape first.

input.command: echo "=== All markdown files ===" && find . -name '*.md' -not -path './build/*' -not -path './install/*' -not -path './log/*' -not -path './.git/*' | sort && echo "" && echo "=== Markdown file sizes (lines) ===" && find . -name '*.md' -not -path './build/*' -not -path './install/*' -not -path './log/*' -not -path './.git/*' -exec wc -l {} \; | sort -rn
input.description: Find all markdown files and their sizes

input.command: echo "=== docs/ tree ===" && find docs -type f 2>/dev/null | sort && echo "" && echo "=== Top-level tree (excl build/install/log/.git) ===" && find . -maxdepth 2 -not -path './build*' -not -path './install*' -not -path './log*' -not -path './.git*' -not -path './src/*' | sort
input.description: Show docs tree and top-level structure

cd /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate
echo "=== Are credential/secret files TRACKED by git? ==="
for f in "Turtlebot-468-Policy" "Turtlebot-468.cert.pem" "Turtlebot-468.private.key" "Turtlebot-468.public.key" "root-CA.crt" "c9109663-destinationAccessToken.txt" "c9109663-sourceAccessToken.txt" "connect_device_package.zip" "start.sh" "rviz/launch_rviz.sh" "rviz/turtlebot468_mapping.rviz"; do
  if git ls-files --error-unmatch "$f" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "TRACKED:   $f"
  else
    ign=$(git check-ignore "$f" 2>/dev/null)
    if [ -n "$ign" ]; then echo "IGNORED:   $f"; else echo "UNTRACKED: $f"; fi
  fi
done

content.text:
This is clearer than the `find` made it look. The key facts:

- **Real tracked repo is only 35 files** — 17 docs (15 in `docs/` + root `README.md`/`CLAUDE.md`), 2 rviz files, and the `guide_mate_explorer` package.
- **The "so many mds" noise is mostly a vendored third-party clone** (`aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/`, ~20 markdowns) that's already gitignored & untracked — clutter on disk, not in the repo.
- **Credentials** (`*.private.key`, certs, access tokens, `connect_device_package.zip`) are present on disk but properly gitignored — I'll verify they were never committed to history.
- **Untracked drift:** new `combined_node.py`, `combined.launch.py`, `slam_fused.yaml`, and a whole new C++ package `src/guide_mate_perception/` not reflected in `CLAUDE.md`.

Now let me run a thorough multi-agent audit of every doc (content, freshness vs. actual code, redundancy, importance) plus repo hygiene, then synthesize a restructure proposal. Let me also verify git history for leaked secrets in parallel.

file.content:
# cs7980-guide-mate

CS7980 project — TurtleBot 4 guide robot that autonomously maps and navigates an indoor space.

This repo holds both the **documentation** (`docs/`) and the **ROS 2 code**
(`src/guide_mate_explorer/`). It is a colcon workspace — see [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) for
build/run/architecture and the hard-won gotchas.

## Code
- [`src/guide_mate_explorer`](src/guide_mate_explorer) — `bfs_explorer` (autonomous BFS
  frontier mapping) and `glass_guard` (bumper-based glass backstop).

```bash
cd ~/cs7980-guide-mate && colcon build --symlink-install
source install/setup.bash
```

## Documentation

See [docs/README.md](docs/README.md) for the full index. Highlights:

- [Autonomous mapping](docs/mapping/README.md) — BFS explorer + SLAM + Nav2, glass handling,
  how to run
- [Depth camera for mapping](docs/mapping/depth-perception.md) — using OAK-D depth to see the
  glass the lidar can't (FOV, height-filtered pipeline, planned lidar-scan injection)
- [Network overview](docs/network/README.md) — two-computer architecture, discovery
  model, addressing, time sync
- [Connecting to NUwave](docs/network/nuwave-connection.md) — step-by-step, including
  the 5 GHz regulatory-domain fix
- [ROS 2 over NUwave](docs/network/ros2-over-nuwave.md) — what works / what doesn't,
  the four root causes, and the working laptop setup
- [OAK-D-LITE camera](docs/camera/oak-d-camera-test.md) — depth/RGB camera test:
  both streams work on USB 2, the USB 3 boot-loop root-caused to power, and the
  bandwidth-limited frame drops

content.content:
1	# cs7980 Guide-Mate — Documentation
2	
3	Working documentation for the cs7980 TurtleBot 4 project.
4	
5	## Index
6	
7	### Network
8	- [Network overview](network/README.md) — how the robots are connected, addressing, discovery model
9	- [Connecting a TurtleBot 4 to NUwave](network/nuwave-connection.md) — step-by-step, including the 5 GHz fix that was the real blocker
10	- [ROS 2 over NUwave — what works and what doesn't](network/ros2-over-nuwave.md) — compatibility findings
11	
12	### Camera
13	- [Camera overview](camera/README.md) — the OAK-D-LITE depth/RGB camera
14	- [OAK-D-LITE camera — test, findings, fixes, pending issues](camera/oak-d-camera-test.md) — both streams work on USB 2; USB 3 boot-loop root-caused to power; bandwidth-limited frame drops
15	
16	### Mapping & navigation
17	- [Autonomous mapping — overview](mapping/README.md) — BFS frontier explorer + SLAM + Nav2, glass handling, how to run, status
18	- [Depth camera for mapping](mapping/depth-perception.md) — using the OAK-D depth to see glass the lidar can't: FOV, the height-filtered pipeline, compute, and the planned lidar-scan injection
19	- [Viewing live mapping in RViz](mapping/rviz-visualization.md) — laptop-side RViz for the map, lidar, depth scan and cloud; the tf-remap, QoS, and Lite-vs-Standard model gotchas
20	- Code: [`src/guide_mate_explorer`](../src/guide_mate_explorer) — the `bfs_explorer` and `glass_guard` nodes
21	
22	### Power & battery
23	- [Power overview](power/README.md) — battery-powered robot; only `battery_state` metering; ~14 W idle
24	- [Power consumption & saving](power/power-saving.md) — measured draw, what's always running, and the working "park" (stop SLAM → lidar auto-idles + CPU freed); what does *not* work
25	
26	### AWS IoT Core (cloud connectivity)
27	- [AWS IoT Core overview](aws-iot/README.md) — what we set up on robot 468, where it could help, and the security note on keeping credentials out of the repo
28	- [What IoT Core is — building blocks](aws-iot/iot-core-overview.md) — things, certs/policies, the MQTT broker, shadows, rules, jobs, Greengrass; how our files map to each
29	- [IoT Core ↔ other AWS services + EC2 access](aws-iot/service-integration.md) — the Rules Engine, service-to-device, EventBridge, and the three ways EC2 can reach IoT Core
30	- [Secure Tunneling — remote SSH to a robot](aws-iot/secure-tunneling.md) — source/destination tokens, `localproxy`, the MQTT auto-delivery path, and why it isn't connected yet
31	
32	---
33	
34	## ⚠️ Security note
35	**Do not commit credentials to this repo.** NUwave usernames/passwords, robot passwords, and any private keys must stay out of version control. The docs use placeholders such as `<nuwave-username>` and `<nuwave-password>`. Keep real values in a local, untracked file (e.g. `secrets.local`, already gitignored) or a password manager.
36	
37	## Quick facts
38	- **Robots:** two TurtleBot 4 units — `turtlebot468` (room 468) and `turtlebot436` (room 436).
39	- **Each robot = two computers:** a Raspberry Pi 4 (Ubuntu 22.04, ROS 2 Humble — the thing you SSH into) and an iRobot Create 3 base (joined to the Pi by USB-C, which is both power and a wired Ethernet link).
40	- **Discovery model:** FastDDS **Discovery Server** running on the Pi; the Create 3 stays off WiFi and talks to the Pi over the USB-C link.
41	- **Default fallback network:** a local ASUS router, SSID `ASUS_98` (the robots auto-connect to it).
42

# cs7980 Guide-Mate — Documentation

Working documentation for the cs7980 TurtleBot 4 project.

## Index

### Network
- [Network overview](network/README.md) — how the robots are connected, addressing, discovery model
- [Connecting a TurtleBot 4 to NUwave](network/nuwave-connection.md) — step-by-step, including the 5 GHz fix that was the real blocker
- [ROS 2 over NUwave — what works and what doesn't](network/ros2-over-nuwave.md) — compatibility findings

### Camera
- [Camera overview](camera/README.md) — the OAK-D-LITE depth/RGB camera
- [OAK-D-LITE camera — test, findings, fixes, pending issues](camera/oak-d-camera-test.md) — both streams work on USB 2; USB 3 boot-loop root-caused to power; bandwidth-limited frame drops

### Mapping & navigation
- [Autonomous mapping — overview](mapping/README.md) — BFS frontier explorer + SLAM + Nav2, glass handling, how to run, status
- [Depth camera for mapping](mapping/depth-perception.md) — using the OAK-D depth to see glass the lidar can't: FOV, the height-filtered pipeline, compute, and the planned lidar-scan injection
- [Viewing live mapping in RViz](mapping/rviz-visualization.md) — laptop-side RViz for the map, lidar, depth scan and cloud; the tf-remap, QoS, and Lite-vs-Standard model gotchas
- Code: [`src/guide_mate_explorer`](../src/guide_mate_explorer) — the `bfs_explorer` and `glass_guard` nodes

### Power & battery
- [Power overview](power/README.md) — battery-powered robot; only `battery_state` metering; ~14 W idle
- [Power consumption & saving](power/power-saving.md) — measured draw, what's always running, and the working "park" (stop SLAM → lidar auto-idles + CPU freed); what does *not* work

### AWS IoT Core (cloud connectivity)
- [AWS IoT Core overview](aws-iot/README.md) — what we set up on robot 468, where it could help, and the security note on keeping credentials out of the repo
- [What IoT Core is — building blocks](aws-iot/iot-core-overview.md) — things, certs/policies, the MQTT broker, shadows, rules, jobs, Greengrass; how our files map to each
- [IoT Core ↔ other AWS services + EC2 access](aws-iot/service-integration.md) — the Rules Engine, service-to-device, EventBridge, and the three ways EC2 can reach IoT Core
- [Secure Tunneling — remote SSH to a robot](aws-iot/secure-tunneling.md) — source/destination tokens, `localproxy`, the MQTT auto-delivery path, and why it isn't connected yet

---

## ⚠️ Security note
**Do not commit credentials to this repo.** NUwave usernames/passwords, robot passwords, and any private keys must stay out of version control. The docs use placeholders such as `<nuwave-username>` and `<nuwave-password>`. Keep real values in a local, untracked file (e.g. `secrets.local`, already gitignored) or a password manager.

## Quick facts
- **Robots:** two TurtleBot 4 units — `turtlebot468` (room 468) and `turtlebot436` (room 436).
- **Each robot = two computers:** a Raspberry Pi 4 (Ubuntu 22.04, ROS 2 Humble — the thing you SSH into) and an iRobot Create 3 base (joined to the Pi by USB-C, which is both power and a wired Ethernet link).
- **Discovery model:** FastDDS **Discovery Server** running on the Pi; the Create 3 stays off WiFi and talks to the Pi over the USB-C link.
- **Default fallback network:** a local ASUS router, SSID `ASUS_98` (the robots auto-connect to it).

4	
5	> ⚠️ **Security:** none of the device credentials live in this repo. The certificate,
6	> private key, root CA, IoT policy, tunnel access tokens, and the AWS "connect a device"
7	> package are **gitignored** (see the repo `.gitignore`). Docs below use placeholders such
8	> as `<account-id>`, `<your-ats-endpoint>`, and `<region>`. The real values for our setup
9	> are in Claude memory (`aws-iot-setup`), not here.
10	
11	## TL;DR
12	- **IoT Core = a managed MQTT message broker + a device security layer**, plus features
13	  built on top (shadows, rules, jobs, secure tunneling).
14	- It does **not** store or analyze data itself — the **Rules Engine** routes messages out
15	  to other AWS services, and **Lambda** is the glue for custom logic.
16	- For the robots it is **robot ↔ cloud** plumbing. It does **not** replace the on-robot
17	  ROS 2 / FastDDS networking (that stays node-to-node; see [network docs](../network/README.md)).
18	- We have the quick-connect MQTT sample working and a **Secure Tunnel** opened, but
19	  `localproxy` is **not yet installed**, so the tunnel is not usable end-to-end yet.
20	
21	## Documents
22	- [What IoT Core is — building blocks](iot-core-overview.md) — things, certs/policies, the MQTT broker, shadows, rules, jobs, Greengrass; and how our files map to each.
23	- [Talking to other AWS services + EC2 access](service-integration.md) — the Rules Engine, service-to-device, EventBridge, and the three ways EC2 can reach IoT Core.

25

27	| Piece | State |
28	|---|---|
29	| Thing in the registry | `Turtlebot-468` (region `<region>`) |
30	| Device cert + private key | downloaded to the Pi (gitignored) |
31	| IoT policy | quick-start scope — **only `sdk/test/*` topics**; no tunnel/shadow topics yet |
32	| MQTT pub/sub sample | runs via `start.sh` (publishes to `sdk/test/python`) |
33	| Secure Tunnel | opened; source + destination tokens downloaded (gitignored) |
34	| `localproxy` | **not installed** — required to actually use the tunnel |
35	
36	## Where IoT Core could help the project
37	| Use case | Feature |
38	|---|---|
39	| SSH into a robot behind the lab firewall, no port-forwarding | **Secure Tunneling** |
40	| Push code/config to both 468 and 436 | **Jobs** |
41	| Stream battery/status telemetry to a dashboard or alert | **Rules Engine → SNS / DynamoDB / Timestream** |
42	| Send a command to a robot from a backend | **publish to an MQTT topic** (Lambda/EC2 via IAM) |
43	
44	See the per-topic docs for detail.

file.content:
# AWS IoT Core — Overview

How AWS IoT Core fits the cs7980 guide-robot project, and what we set up on robot **468**.

> ⚠️ **Security:** none of the device credentials live in this repo. The certificate,
> private key, root CA, IoT policy, tunnel access tokens, and the AWS "connect a device"
> package are **gitignored** (see the repo `.gitignore`). Docs below use placeholders such
> as `<account-id>`, `<your-ats-endpoint>`, and `<region>`. The real values for our setup
> are in Claude memory (`aws-iot-setup`), not here.

## TL;DR
- **IoT Core = a managed MQTT message broker + a device security layer**, plus features
  built on top (shadows, rules, jobs, secure tunneling).
- It does **not** store or analyze data itself — the **Rules Engine** routes messages out
  to other AWS services, and **Lambda** is the glue for custom logic.
- For the robots it is **robot ↔ cloud** plumbing. It does **not** replace the on-robot
  ROS 2 / FastDDS networking (that stays node-to-node; see [network docs](../network/README.md)).
- We have the quick-connect MQTT sample working and a **Secure Tunnel** opened, but
  `localproxy` is **not yet installed**, so the tunnel is not usable end-to-end yet.

## Documents
- [What IoT Core is — building blocks](iot-core-overview.md) — things, certs/policies, the MQTT broker, shadows, rules, jobs, Greengrass; and how our files map to each.
- [Talking to other AWS services + EC2 access](service-integration.md) — the Rules Engine, service-to-device, EventBridge, and the three ways EC2 can reach IoT Core.
- [Secure Tunneling — remote SSH to a robot](secure-tunneling.md) — source/destination tokens, `localproxy`, the MQTT auto-delivery path, and why it isn't connected yet.

## What we set up (robot 468)
| Piece | State |
|---|---|
| Thing in the registry | `Turtlebot-468` (region `<region>`) |
| Device cert + private key | downloaded to the Pi (gitignored) |
| IoT policy | quick-start scope — **only `sdk/test/*` topics**; no tunnel/shadow topics yet |
| MQTT pub/sub sample | runs via `start.sh` (publishes to `sdk/test/python`) |
| Secure Tunnel | opened; source + destination tokens downloaded (gitignored) |
| `localproxy` | **not installed** — required to actually use the tunnel |

## Where IoT Core could help the project
| Use case | Feature |
|---|---|
| SSH into a robot behind the lab firewall, no port-forwarding | **Secure Tunneling** |
| Push code/config to both 468 and 436 | **Jobs** |
| Stream battery/status telemetry to a dashboard or alert | **Rules Engine → SNS / DynamoDB / Timestream** |
| Send a command to a robot from a backend | **publish to an MQTT topic** (Lambda/EC2 via IAM) |

See the per-topic docs for detail.

content.content:
1	# Camera
2	
3	This section documents the robot's depth/RGB camera (Luxonis **OAK-D-LITE**).
4	
5	## Documents
6	- [OAK-D-LITE camera — test, findings, fixes, pending issues](oak-d-camera-test.md)
7	
8	## Quick facts
9	- The OAK-D-LITE provides **both** the RGB "webcam" and the stereo **depth** camera —
10	  there is no separate USB webcam (`/dev/video*` are the Pi's internal codec/ISP nodes).
11	- It is driven through ROS 2 via `depthai_ros_driver` (`ros-humble-depthai`), **not** as a
12	  UVC device. There is no standalone Python `depthai`.
13	- Topics: `/camera/rgb/image_raw` (`bgr8`) and `/camera/stereo/image_raw` (`16UC1`),
14	  both published with **`BEST_EFFORT`** sensor QoS.
15	- **Known issue:** on default settings the camera enters a USB 3 boot/disconnect loop
16	  caused by **power starvation** on the Pi 4. Workaround is to force USB 2
17	  (`-p camera.i_usb_speed:=HIGH`), which is stable but bandwidth-limited (~80 % frame
18	  drops at 30 fps). Proper fix — independent camera power — is **pending**. See the
19	  document above.
20

file.content:
# Camera

This section documents the robot's depth/RGB camera (Luxonis **OAK-D-LITE**).

## Documents
- [OAK-D-LITE camera — test, findings, fixes, pending issues](oak-d-camera-test.md)

## Quick facts
- The OAK-D-LITE provides **both** the RGB "webcam" and the stereo **depth** camera —
  there is no separate USB webcam (`/dev/video*` are the Pi's internal codec/ISP nodes).
- It is driven through ROS 2 via `depthai_ros_driver` (`ros-humble-depthai`), **not** as a
  UVC device. There is no standalone Python `depthai`.
- Topics: `/camera/rgb/image_raw` (`bgr8`) and `/camera/stereo/image_raw` (`16UC1`),
  both published with **`BEST_EFFORT`** sensor QoS.
- **Known issue:** on default settings the camera enters a USB 3 boot/disconnect loop
  caused by **power starvation** on the Pi 4. Workaround is to force USB 2
  (`-p camera.i_usb_speed:=HIGH`), which is stable but bandwidth-limited (~80 % frame
  drops at 30 fps). Proper fix — independent camera power — is **pending**. See the
  document above.

content.content:
1	# Power & Battery
2	
3	Robot 468 is battery-powered (iRobot Create 3 base battery feeds both the Create 3 *and* the Raspberry Pi 4 over the USB-C link). The full perception/SLAM stack runs continuously regardless of motion, so understanding — and reducing — idle draw matters for runtime.
4	
5	## Index
6	- [Power consumption & saving](power-saving.md) — measured idle draw, what's always running and why, and the **one working way to park the robot** (stop SLAM → lidar auto-idles + CPU freed). Includes what does *not* work.
7	
8	## Quick facts
9	- **Only power sensor on the robot:** `/turtlebot468/battery_state` (whole-pack, aggregate — no per-component metering exists on stock TB4).
10	- **Measured idle draw (undocked, stationary):** **~14 W** (−1.0 A @ ~14.15 V).
11	- **Pack:** ~1.968 Ah / ~14.4 V ≈ 28 Wh → roughly **2 h** idle runtime from full, before driving.
12	- **Battery current sign:** negative = discharging (real load, only visible **undocked**); positive = charging.
13

file.content:
# Power & Battery

Robot 468 is battery-powered (iRobot Create 3 base battery feeds both the Create 3 *and* the Raspberry Pi 4 over the USB-C link). The full perception/SLAM stack runs continuously regardless of motion, so understanding — and reducing — idle draw matters for runtime.

## Index
- [Power consumption & saving](power-saving.md) — measured idle draw, what's always running and why, and the **one working way to park the robot** (stop SLAM → lidar auto-idles + CPU freed). Includes what does *not* work.

## Quick facts
- **Only power sensor on the robot:** `/turtlebot468/battery_state` (whole-pack, aggregate — no per-component metering exists on stock TB4).
- **Measured idle draw (undocked, stationary):** **~14 W** (−1.0 A @ ~14.15 V).
- **Pack:** ~1.968 Ah / ~14.4 V ≈ 28 Wh → roughly **2 h** idle runtime from full, before driving.
- **Battery current sign:** negative = discharging (real load, only visible **undocked**); positive = charging.

content.content:
1	# Autonomous mapping — overview
2	
3	How robot **468** maps an indoor space on its own, and how we handle obstacles the 2D
4	lidar can't see (glass). Code lives in [`src/guide_mate_explorer`](../../src/guide_mate_explorer).
5	
6	**TL;DR:** A custom **BFS frontier explorer** drives the robot to unexplored areas while
7	`slam_toolbox` builds the map and Nav2 plans/avoids. Because the 2D lidar is blind to
8	**glass**, we add the **OAK-D depth camera** (which sees the glass doors' metal base) as a
9	costmap obstacle source, with the **Create 3 bumper** as a last-resort backstop.
10	
11	---
12	
13	## The stack
14	
15	```
16	                 ┌────────────── sensors ──────────────┐
17	   RPLIDAR (360°, ~8 Hz) ──► /scan ──┐                  OAK-D depth ──► (see depth-perception.md)
18	   Create 3 base ──► /odom, /tf      │                       │
19	                                     ▼                       ▼
20	                              slam_toolbox            depth obstacles
21	                          (lidar, builds /map)        (glass metal base,
22	                                     │                  low objects, overhangs)
23	                        map + map→odom                       │
24	                                     ▼                       ▼
25	                                   Nav2  ◄── costmaps (lidar scan + depth scan + bump layer)
26	                                     │
27	                            navigate_to_pose
28	                                     ▲
29	                              bfs_explorer ──► picks nearest frontier, drives there, repeats
30	                                     ▲
31	                              glass_guard  ──► on bump: persistent costmap mark + blacklist
32	```
33	
34	## Components (all in `guide_mate_explorer`)
35	
36	| Piece | What it does |
37	|---|---|
38	| `bfs_explorer` | BFS from the robot's cell through free space to the **nearest frontier** (free cell touching unknown). Sends it to Nav2, replans on arrival/failure, auto-saves the map, blacklists dead ends, stops when no frontiers remain. "BFS manner" = nearest-frontier-first. |
39	| `glass_guard` | Subscribes to the Create 3 bumper; on **BUMP**, projects the bumper into `map`, republishes a **non-clearing** `PointCloud2` (so the lidar can't erase it), and publishes the hit so the explorer blacklists it. |
40	| depth pipeline | OAK-D depth → 3D pointcloud → **height-filtered** 2D scan (`oakd/scan`) → 2nd costmap obstacle source. Catches the glass doors' metal base. See [depth-perception.md](depth-perception.md). |
41	| `depth_lidar_fusion` | The alternative to the separate `oakd/scan` source: **collapses** depth per column to the nearest non-floor point and **min-injects** it into the lidar scan → `scan_fused`. Point SLAM + Nav2 at `scan_fused` and the glass also lands in the **SLAM map**. Only lowers beams, never erases lidar; falls back to raw lidar if depth goes stale. Built + offline-validated. See [depth-perception.md](depth-perception.md). |
42	| `nav2_glass.yaml` | Nav2 config: `oakd/scan` as a 2nd obstacle source in both costmaps, the non-clearing bump layer, speed capped to 0.15 m/s. |
43	
44	## Why glass is the hard part
45	The RPLIDAR scans a single horizontal plane (~0.19 m). **Glass is transparent to it**, so to
46	Nav2 a glass wall looks like open space — and the robot drove into one. The fix is layered:
47	
48	1. **Depth camera** sees the glass doors' **metal base/frame** (below the lidar plane) → costmap obstacle → planner routes around it. *Proactive, but only where a frame is visible.*
49	2. **`glass_guard` / bumper** catches fully-transparent sections with no visible frame and marks them **permanently**. *Reactive last resort.*
50	3. **`depth_lidar_fusion`** injects the depth scan into the lidar scan (`scan_fused`) so glass also lands in the **SLAM map**, not just the runtime costmap. *Built + offline-validated; on-robot localization check pending.*
51	
52	> Industry-standard alternatives for known glass: pre-drawn **keepout-zone** map masks and **ultrasonic** sensors (sonar reflects off glass). The TB4 has no ultrasonics; keepout zones are a possible future add.
53	
54	## How to run
55	
56	```bash
57	source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash
58	source ~/cs7980-guide-mate/install/setup.bash
59	
60	# 1) OAK-D depth on USB2 (see ../camera/oak-d-camera-test.md for why USB2)
61	ros2 run depthai_ros_driver camera_node --ros-args \
62	  -r __ns:=/turtlebot468 -r __node:=oakd \
63	  -p camera.i_pipeline_type:=RGBD -p camera.i_usb_speed:=HIGH -p camera.i_nn_type:=none \
64	  -p camera.i_enable_imu:=false -p rgb.i_publish_topic:=false -p rgb.i_enable_preview:=false \
65	  -p left.i_fps:=6.0 -p right.i_fps:=6.0 \
66	  -p stereo.i_publish_topic:=true -p stereo.i_align_depth:=false -p stereo.i_resolution:=400
67	
68	# 2) Depth perception (pointcloud + height-filtered scan)
69	ros2 launch guide_mate_explorer depth_perception.launch.py namespace:=turtlebot468
70	
71	# 3) Full autonomous mapping — STARTS MOTION; make sure the area is clear
72	ros2 launch guide_mate_explorer autonomous_mapping.launch.py namespace:=turtlebot468
73	
74	# watch progress / completion / saved map
75	tail -f /tmp/explore.log
76	ros2 topic echo /turtlebot468/exploration_complete
77	ls -la ~/maps/
78	```
79	
80	To **watch** the map, lidar, depth scan and cloud build live from a laptop, see
81	[Viewing live mapping in RViz](rviz-visualization.md).
82	
83	## Hard-won constraints (read before debugging)
84	- **Namespaced TF** needs `('/tf','tf'),('/tf_static','tf_static')` remaps or lookups fail silently. See [`turtlebot4-namespaced-tf-remap`](../../CLAUDE.md).
85	- **Pi 4 is compute-bound** — SLAM ≈ 1 core; running SLAM + full depth pointcloud + Nav2 together saturates it. We freed ~25% by killing diagnostics/joy/teleop. The fast depth-collapse (see depth doc) is the on-robot answer; offloading to a laptop is the long-term one.
86	- **Create 3 hazards** are event-only on the raw `_do_not_use` topic.
87	- Robot speed is capped to **0.15 m/s** during mapping because the depth scan is currently slow.
88	
89	## Status / roadmap
90	- ✅ Explorer, glass_guard, depth pipeline built; depth **validated to detect the glass metal base**; migrated into this repo.
91	- ✅ `depth_lidar_fusion` (fast collapse + inject into lidar scan → glass in the SLAM map) **built + offline-validated** (synthetic wall: per-column collapse, ±38° wedge, min-inject only lowers beams, graceful stale-depth fallback).
92	- ⏭️ On robot: eyeball `scan` vs `scan_fused` in RViz → point SLAM + Nav2 at `scan_fused` → localization sanity check → full mapping run.
93	- ⏳ Not yet: keepout-zone masks; offload heavy nodes to a laptop; repeat on robot 436.
94

file.content:
# Autonomous mapping — overview

How robot **468** maps an indoor space on its own, and how we handle obstacles the 2D
lidar can't see (glass). Code lives in [`src/guide_mate_explorer`](../../src/guide_mate_explorer).

**TL;DR:** A custom **BFS frontier explorer** drives the robot to unexplored areas while
`slam_toolbox` builds the map and Nav2 plans/avoids. Because the 2D lidar is blind to
**glass**, we add the **OAK-D depth camera** (which sees the glass doors' metal base) as a
costmap obstacle source, with the **Create 3 bumper** as a last-resort backstop.

---

## The stack

```
                 ┌────────────── sensors ──────────────┐
   RPLIDAR (360°, ~8 Hz) ──► /scan ──┐                  OAK-D depth ──► (see depth-perception.md)
   Create 3 base ──► /odom, /tf      │                       │
                                     ▼                       ▼
                              slam_toolbox            depth obstacles
                          (lidar, builds /map)        (glass metal base,
                                     │                  low objects, overhangs)
                        map + map→odom                       │
                                     ▼                       ▼
                                   Nav2  ◄── costmaps (lidar scan + depth scan + bump layer)
                                     │
                            navigate_to_pose
                                     ▲
                              bfs_explorer ──► picks nearest frontier, drives there, repeats
                                     ▲
                              glass_guard  ──► on bump: persistent costmap mark + blacklist
```

## Components (all in `guide_mate_explorer`)

| Piece | What it does |
|---|---|
| `bfs_explorer` | BFS from the robot's cell through free space to the **nearest frontier** (free cell touching unknown). Sends it to Nav2, replans on arrival/failure, auto-saves the map, blacklists dead ends, stops when no frontiers remain. "BFS manner" = nearest-frontier-first. |
| `glass_guard` | Subscribes to the Create 3 bumper; on **BUMP**, projects the bumper into `map`, republishes a **non-clearing** `PointCloud2` (so the lidar can't erase it), and publishes the hit so the explorer blacklists it. |
| depth pipeline | OAK-D depth → 3D pointcloud → **height-filtered** 2D scan (`oakd/scan`) → 2nd costmap obstacle source. Catches the glass doors' metal base. See [depth-perception.md](depth-perception.md). |
| `depth_lidar_fusion` | The alternative to the separate `oakd/scan` source: **collapses** depth per column to the nearest non-floor point and **min-injects** it into the lidar scan → `scan_fused`. Point SLAM + Nav2 at `scan_fused` and the glass also lands in the **SLAM map**. Only lowers beams, never erases lidar; falls back to raw lidar if depth goes stale. Built + offline-validated. See [depth-perception.md](depth-perception.md). |
| `nav2_glass.yaml` | Nav2 config: `oakd/scan` as a 2nd obstacle source in both costmaps, the non-clearing bump layer, speed capped to 0.15 m/s. |

## Why glass is the hard part
The RPLIDAR scans a single horizontal plane (~0.19 m). **Glass is transparent to it**, so to
Nav2 a glass wall looks like open space — and the robot drove into one. The fix is layered:

1. **Depth camera** sees the glass doors' **metal base/frame** (below the lidar plane) → costmap obstacle → planner routes around it. *Proactive, but only where a frame is visible.*
2. **`glass_guard` / bumper** catches fully-transparent sections with no visible frame and marks them **permanently**. *Reactive last resort.*
3. **`depth_lidar_fusion`** injects the depth scan into the lidar scan (`scan_fused`) so glass also lands in the **SLAM map**, not just the runtime costmap. *Built + offline-validated; on-robot localization check pending.*

> Industry-standard alternatives for known glass: pre-drawn **keepout-zone** map masks and **ultrasonic** sensors (sonar reflects off glass). The TB4 has no ultrasonics; keepout zones are a possible future add.

## How to run

```bash
source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash
source ~/cs7980-guide-mate/install/setup.bash

# 1) OAK-D depth on USB2 (see ../camera/oak-d-camera-test.md for why USB2)
ros2 run depthai_ros_driver camera_node --ros-args \
  -r __ns:=/turtlebot468 -r __node:=oakd \
  -p camera.i_pipeline_type:=RGBD -p camera.i_usb_speed:=HIGH -p camera.i_nn_type:=none \
  -p camera.i_enable_imu:=false -p rgb.i_publish_topic:=false -p rgb.i_enable_preview:=false \
  -p left.i_fps:=6.0 -p right.i_fps:=6.0 \
  -p stereo.i_publish_topic:=true -p stereo.i_align_depth:=false -p stereo.i_resolution:=400

# 2) Depth perception (pointcloud + height-filtered scan)
ros2 launch guide_mate_explorer depth_perception.launch.py namespace:=turtlebot468

# 3) Full autonomous mapping — STARTS MOTION; make sure the area is clear
ros2 launch guide_mate_explorer autonomous_mapping.launch.py namespace:=turtlebot468

# watch progress / completion / saved map
tail -f /tmp/explore.log
ros2 topic echo /turtlebot468/exploration_complete
ls -la ~/maps/
```

To **watch** the map, lidar, depth scan and cloud build live from a laptop, see
[Viewing live mapping in RViz](rviz-visualization.md).

## Hard-won constraints (read before debugging)
- **Namespaced TF** needs `('/tf','tf'),('/tf_static','tf_static')` remaps or lookups fail silently. See [`turtlebot4-namespaced-tf-remap`](../../CLAUDE.md).
- **Pi 4 is compute-bound** — SLAM ≈ 1 core; running SLAM + full depth pointcloud + Nav2 together saturates it. We freed ~25% by killing diagnostics/joy/teleop. The fast depth-collapse (see depth doc) is the on-robot answer; offloading to a laptop is the long-term one.
- **Create 3 hazards** are event-only on the raw `_do_not_use` topic.
- Robot speed is capped to **0.15 m/s** during mapping because the depth scan is currently slow.

## Status / roadmap
- ✅ Explorer, glass_guard, depth pipeline built; depth **validated to detect the glass metal base**; migrated into this repo.
- ✅ `depth_lidar_fusion` (fast collapse + inject into lidar scan → glass in the SLAM map) **built + offline-validated** (synthetic wall: per-column collapse, ±38° wedge, min-inject only lowers beams, graceful stale-depth fallback).
- ⏭️ On robot: eyeball `scan` vs `scan_fused` in RViz → point SLAM + Nav2 at `scan_fused` → localization sanity check → full mapping run.
- ⏳ Not yet: keepout-zone masks; offload heavy nodes to a laptop; repeat on robot 436.

file.content:
# Network Overview

This section documents the network setup for the cs7980 TurtleBot 4 robots.

## Documents
- [Connecting a TurtleBot 4 to NUwave](nuwave-connection.md)
- [ROS 2 over NUwave — what works and what doesn't](ros2-over-nuwave.md)

## The two-computer architecture
A TurtleBot 4 is **two networked computers**:

| Component | Role | WiFi capability |
|---|---|---|
| **Raspberry Pi 4** | Runs Ubuntu 22.04 + ROS 2 Humble. The host you SSH into. | 2.4 + 5 GHz (dual-band) |
| **iRobot Create 3 base** | Mobility base (wheels, IMU, bumpers, docking). | 2.4 GHz only, **WPA2-Personal only** |

They are joined by the **USB-C cable**, which carries both power and a **wired Ethernet link** on the `192.168.186.0/24` subnet (Pi = `.3`, Create 3 = `.2`).

### Why the Create 3 never joins NUwave
The Create 3's firmware does **not** support WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X / PEAP) — it can only join a network with a single shared password. NUwave is enterprise, so the Create 3 **cannot** join it. This is fine because the robots use **Discovery Server** mode: the Create 3 keeps WiFi disabled and routes everything through the Pi over the USB-C link. Only the **Pi** connects to NUwave.

## Discovery model: FastDDS Discovery Server
ROS 2's default discovery is **multicast**, which is link-local and **will not cross IP subnets**. On NUwave the Pi and a laptop typically land on **different `/19` segments**, so multicast discovery is a non-starter.

The robots instead run a **FastDDS Discovery Server** on the Pi (`fast-discovery-server -i 0 -p 11811`, listening on `0.0.0.0:11811`). This uses **unicast**, which routes across subnets. Clients (the Pi's own ROS nodes, and any laptop) point at the server via `ROS_DISCOVERY_SERVER`.

Relevant environment on the Pi (`/etc/turtlebot4/setup.bash`):
```
ROS_DOMAIN_ID=0
RMW_IMPLEMENTATION=rmw_fastrtps_cpp
ROS_LOCALHOST_ONLY=0
ROS_DISCOVERY_SERVER=127.0.0.1:11811;
FASTRTPS_DEFAULT_PROFILES_FILE=/etc/turtlebot4/fastdds_rpi.xml
```
> **Cleaned up 2026-06-12:** stale `192.168.50.x` entries from a previous network were
> removed from three places on robot 468: the Pi's `ROS_DISCOVERY_SERVER`
> (`192.168.50.31:11811`), the **Create 3's own discovery-server address** (same stale
> IP — this one was *not* harmless: it silenced every base topic; see
> [ros2-over-nuwave.md](ros2-over-nuwave.md)), and chrony's preferred NTP server
> (`192.168.50.146` → `pool.ntp.org`, verified working over NUwave). After any future
> network change, check for relics: `grep -r "192.168.50" /etc/`
> Robot **436 has not been migrated or cleaned up yet** — same steps apply.

## Time sync
Chain: internet NTP (`pool.ntp.org`, with `ntp.ubuntu.com` backup) → Pi (chrony) →
Create 3 (chrony serves `192.168.186.0/24`). **Gotcha:** the Pi has **no RTC** — it
boots with a stale clock (whatever it was at last shutdown) until chrony steps it once
WiFi connects. Services started before the step see a huge time jump; check
`chronyc tracking` when debugging anything time-related.

## Addressing summary (as configured June 2026)
| Host | NUwave IP (example) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `turtlebot468` Pi | `10.247.204.21/19` | DHCP — will change |
| Create 3 (468) | `192.168.186.2` | wired-only, via Pi |
| Lab laptop | `10.247.228.155/19` | different `/19` than the Pi |

> NUwave IPs are DHCP and **change** on reconnect. The robot shows its current IP on its display. On the lab `ASUS_98` network the Pis were previously `10.91.221.14` (468) and `10.91.221.95` (436).

content.text: These docs are genuinely high-quality — the real issue is structural, not content quality. I can already see the pattern: heavy redundancy in "Quick facts" repeated across `CLAUDE.md`, `docs/README.md`, and each thin section `README.md` stub; over-fragmentation into single-file subdirectories; and an outsized AWS-IoT section for something "not connected yet." Let me check the untracked code drift myself while the workflow runs.

content.text:
I now have an independent read on the code drift, which confirms a real **docs-vs-code gap** beyond just structure:

- `combined_node.py` → new `guide_mate_bringup` entry point (runs `glass_guard` + `bfs_explorer` in one process to pay rclpy/DDS overhead once; deliberately excludes the GIL-bound fusion node).
- `guide_mate_perception/` → a **second colcon package**, a C++ port of `depth_lidar_fusion` (~10× cheaper CPU — this is the answer to the Pi-4 compute-saturation problem).
- `slam_fused.yaml` → new config (SLAM pointed at `scan_fused`, the roadmap's "Next" item).

None of these appear in `CLAUDE.md`, which still describes one package / 3 nodes / 4 launch files.

The audit workflow is running in the background (auditing all 17 docs in parallel + hygiene + code-reality, then synthesize → adversarial critique → final plan). I'll present the consolidated restructure proposal — cross-checked against my own reading above — as soon as it lands.

# Documentation & Workspace Restructure Plan — cs7980-guide-mate

## Diagnosis (what's wrong today)

The repo is **substantively healthy** — every hardware finding, IP, gotcha, and validation number is accurate and irreplaceable. The problems are **structure, freshness, and on-disk hygiene**, not content.

1. **Docs over-fragment.** Five `docs/` subdirs, each fronted by a section-index README. Two of them — `camera/` and `power/` — wrap a *single* content file behind a 12–19 line near-empty index, adding a directory hop for zero information. `mapping/`, `network/`, and `aws-iot/` each hold 3 substantial files and genuinely earn their index.

2. **Three authoritative docs have drifted from code.** `CLAUDE.md`, `README.md`, and `docs/mapping/` still describe the retired `oakd/scan` "separate 2nd costmap source" design. The active configs (`nav2_glass.yaml` + `slam_fused.yaml`) have migrated to **`scan_fused` as the single fused source for BOTH Nav2 and SLAM**. They also omit post-commit work: the `guide_mate_perception` C++ port, `combined_node`/`guide_mate_bringup`, and `slam_fused.yaml`.

3. **On-disk clutter dominates the working tree.** A **5.4 MB vendored AWS SDK clone** (`aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/`, 2.9 MB is its own nested `.git` — gitignored, not a submodule); **loose AWS credentials + 2 expired tunnel tokens at repo root**; and inconsistent run-helper handling (`rviz/*.sh` tracked, `start.sh` gitignored). Worse: **`start.sh` actively re-clones the SDK at runtime** (lines 18–21) and `pip install`s the local copy (line 26) — so deleting the in-tree clone without fixing the script regenerates the clutter on the next run.

4. **A split commit breaks fresh builds.** `setup.py` (tracked) already declares `guide_mate_bringup = guide_mate_explorer.combined_node:main` (line 33), but `combined_node.py` is **untracked** — a fresh `clone + colcon build` produces a broken entry point. The `guide_mate_perception/` C++ package is also fully untracked. This is a build-correctness issue to resolve **before** any restructure lands.

**No credential was ever committed — git history is clean.** This is hygiene, not exposure remediation.

---

## Proposed structure (new repo top-level + `docs/`)

```
cs7980-guide-mate/                  # colcon workspace root
├── CLAUDE.md                       # KEEP authoritative; refresh: scan_fused, 2 pkgs, slam_fused.yaml, combined runner
├── README.md                       # KEEP front-door; fix inventory (depth_lidar_fusion, 2nd pkg, aws-iot/ + power/ links)
├── .gitignore                      # UPDATE: add /secrets/; KEEP cred globs as defense-in-depth; drop /aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/ and start.sh lines
├── docs/
│   ├── README.md                   # KEEP docs index + security policy; fix secrets.local→secrets/; relink camera.md & power.md
│   ├── camera.md                   # MERGE  camera/README.md (thin) → camera/oak-d-camera-test.md → ONE file
│   ├── power.md                    # MERGE  power/README.md (thin)  → power/power-saving.md      → ONE file
│   ├── mapping/                    # KEEP subdir (3 substantial files — earns it)
│   │   ├── README.md               # UPDATE: scan_fused single source; add C++ port + combined-runner rows; advance status
│   │   ├── depth-perception.md     # UPDATE: add "C++ port (guide_mate_perception)" subsection; mark fusion hw-validated
│   │   └── rviz-visualization.md   # KEEP (verified accurate); fix one path ref → scripts/launch_rviz.sh
│   ├── network/                    # KEEP subdir as-is (3 substantial, verified-accurate files)
│   │   ├── README.md
│   │   ├── nuwave-connection.md
│   │   └── ros2-over-nuwave.md
│   └── aws-iot/                    # KEEP subdir as-is (3 content files + index — justified)
│       ├── README.md               # KEEP (sole home of robot-468 IoT setup-state table); add awsiotsdk==1.30.0 pin
│       ├── iot-core-overview.md
│       ├── service-integration.md
│       └── secure-tunneling.md
├── scripts/                        # NEW: group loose run-helpers
│   ├── launch_rviz.sh              # MOVE from rviz/ (tracked)
│   └── start.sh.example            # NEW sanitized template (env-driven paths; no in-tree SDK clone; pip awsiotsdk)
├── rviz/
│   └── turtlebot468_mapping.rviz   # KEEP (tracked RViz config — data, not a script)
└── src/                            # colcon src — TWO packages now
    ├── guide_mate_explorer/        # Python pkg — commit combined_node.py + combined.launch.py + setup.py line together
    └── guide_mate_perception/      # C++ fusion port — commit as a complete unit (CMakeLists/package.xml/src/launch)

── gitignored, NOT in tree ──────────────────────────────────────────────
   ├── secrets/                     # NEW consolidated creds dir (whole dir ignored) — keys/certs/policy/CA/tokens/zip
   └── ~/vendor/aws-iot-sdk/        # vendored SDK relocated OUT of workspace (optional); docs pin awsiotsdk==1.30.0
```

---

## Per-file plan

Covers **every** tracked doc plus the structural moves.

| File | Action | Destination | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| `CLAUDE.md` | **update** | — | Irreplaceable agent brief (8 gotchas, OAK bring-up incantation, hw-validation numbers). Refresh stale inventory — see **CLAUDE.md sync** below. |

| `docs/README.md` | **update** | — | Docs landing + sole credentials-policy statement. Fix security note: it cites `secrets.local` (no such entry exists) → point at the new gitignored `secrets/` dir. Update node list (add `depth_lidar_fusion` + combined runner). Relink merged `camera.md`/`power.md`. |
| `docs/camera/README.md` | **merge** | `docs/camera.md` | 19-line near-empty index over one content file. Fold its one nugget (`/dev/video*` are Pi codec nodes, no standalone python depthai) into the top of the content file, then delete the subdir. |
| `docs/camera/oak-d-camera-test.md` | **rename** | `docs/camera.md` | Becomes the single camera doc. Irreplaceable hardware evidence (MXID `18443010B112591200`, USB IDs, throttle bits, bandwidth math, Y-adapter fix). Apply the one-pass naming/size reconciliation (see cleanup §6). |
| `docs/power/README.md` | **merge** | `docs/power.md` | 12-line index; every fact is verbatim-duplicated from `power-saving.md` and already in `docs/README.md`. Collapse the dir; fold intro into the content file. |
| `docs/power/power-saving.md` | **rename** | `docs/power.md` | Single power doc. Irreplaceable empirical findings (measured −1.0 A / ~14 W draw, working park method, lidar `auto_standby`, soft-park failure modes). |
| `docs/mapping/README.md` | **update** | — | Drifted. Rewrite `nav2_glass.yaml` row + stack diagram to **`scan_fused` single fused source**; fix "How to run" (`autonomous_mapping` self-starts camera+fusion+SLAM+Nav2; `depth_perception` step is vestigial); advance status (scan_fused wired + fusion hw-validated, not pending); remove the unimplemented `/tmp/explore.log` tail; add rows for combined runner + C++ fusion node. |
| `docs/mapping/depth-perception.md` | **update** | — | Canonical fusion writeup. Add **"C++ port (`guide_mate_perception`)"** subsection (motivation: Pi-4 CPU, ~10× cheaper; numerical-equivalence notes: float32/`lrint`/median/tie-break; `depth_lidar_fusion_cpp.launch.py`; run Python OR C++, same node name). Mark fusion **hardware-validated** (205 seen / 195 inject

**Confirmed on disk:** `aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/` (5.4 MB clone), 4 credential files + `connect_device_package.zip` + `root-CA.crt` at root, 2 `c9109663-*AccessToken.txt`, and `start.sh` that re-clones + local-pip-installs the SDK.

1. **Remove the vendored SDK — AND stop the script from regenerating it.** Delete `aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/` (5.4 MB; 2.9 MB nested `.git` confuses grep/IDE indexers). **This is not enough on its own:** `start.sh` lines 18–21 re-`git clone --recursive` it into the workspace cwd and line 26 `pip install`s the local copy. The sanitized `scripts/start.sh.example` **must remove the clone-and-local-pip-install block** and instead depend on a pip-installed `awsiotsdk==1.30.0` (PyPI name). Ignore the **whole directory** (`/secrets/`). Point the bring-up script at the new path via env (`$IOT_CERT`, `$IOT_KEY`, `$IOT_CA`). History is clean — hygiene, not remediation.

3. **Delete the two expired tunnel tokens** `c9109663-destinationAccessToken.txt` and `c9109663-sourceAccessToken.txt` — single-use/short-lived, never usable (localproxy was never installed), now stale junk.

4. **`.gitignore` reconciliation (3 edits):**
   - **Add** `/secrets/` (one whole-dir line — closes the rename-bypass gap, e.g. `cert.pem.old`, `backup.key.bak`).
   - **Keep** the filename-shaped globs (`*.pem`, `*.key`, `*.crt`, `*-Policy`, `*AccessToken*`, `connect_device_package.zip`) as **defense-in-depth** — a conscious decision, not half-redundancy.
   - **Drop** the now-stale `/aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/` line (clone relocated out of tree) and the `start.sh` line (script moves to `scripts/start.sh` or `scripts/start.sh.example`).

5. **Resolve `start.sh` handling.** Currently gitignored (line 23) and invisible to collaborators, yet it's the documented bring-up convenience. Either way it moves under `scripts/` so root stops mixing README/CLAUDE with creds, the SDK clone, and a loose script.

6. **One-pass OAK naming/size reconciliation (do as a discrete step, not opportunistically).** The **OAK-D vs OAK-D-LITE** name and the **depth-image-size** facts (`640×480` actual vs `1280×720` `camera_info` vs `640×400`) are inconsistent **repo-wide**. The `camera_info` trap (depthai reports 1280×720 but the image is 640×480) stays documented as the *reason*.

Flagging so the move doesn't stall.)*

**Preserved (irreplaceable — untouched in content):** all 8 CLAUDE.md gotchas + OAK bring-up incantation; every hardware datum (MXID, USB IDs, bandwidth math, −1.0 A/~14 W power, 205-seen/195-injected fusion numbers); all network root-causes (5 GHz regdom, WPA2-Enterprise profile, discovery-server debugging trail, `192.168.186.0/24` layout); the robot-468 IoT setup-state table; the RViz operator knowledge (Lite-vs-Standard URDF, benign-log list). **Risks (and mitigations):**
- **start.sh regenerates clutter.** Deleting the in-tree SDK without rewriting `start.sh` (lines 18–21, 26) re-creates the 5.4 MB clone on next run. *Mitigation: the script rewrite is part of the cleanup, not optional.*

- **Over-claiming validation.** Marking fusion hardware-validated and committing the new code could imply the on-robot localization check passed. *Mitigation: keep the "on-robot SLAM-map sanity check pending" caveat in CLAUDE.md and mapping docs.*
- **Naming/size piecemeal drift.** Fixing OAK-D/Lite and 640×480/1280×720/640×400 one file at a time invites new inconsistency. *Mitigation: do it as the single discrete one-pass sweep across CLAUDE.md + oakd_mapping.yaml + camera.md (cleanup §6).*
- **Convention asymmetry.** Single-file docs alongside index-fronted dirs diverges from the current uniform "every dir has a README" pattern. *Acceptable for single-doc topics; call it out in the PR so reviewers expecting symmetry understand the multi-file dirs intentionally keep their indexes.*
- **`root:root` ownership on `docs/camera/`** may cause edit/move friction. *Mitigation: one-time `sudo chown` before the merge.*",

---

# Documentation & Workspace Restructure Plan — cs7980-guide-mate

## Diagnosis (what's wrong today)

The repo is **substantively healthy** — every hardware finding, IP, gotcha, and validation number is accurate and irreplaceable. The problems are **structure, freshness, and on-disk hygiene**, not content.

1. **Docs over-fragment.** Five `docs/` subdirs, each fronted by a section-index README. Two of them — `camera/` and `power/` — wrap a *single* content file behind a 12–19 line near-empty index, adding a directory hop for zero information. `mapping/`, `network/`, and `aws-iot/` each hold 3 substantial files and genuinely earn their index.

2. **Three authoritative docs have drifted from code.** `CLAUDE.md`, `README.md`, and `docs/mapping/` still describe the retired `oakd/scan` "separate 2nd costmap source" design. The active configs (`nav2_glass.yaml` + `slam_fused.yaml`) have migrated to **`scan_fused` as the single fused source for BOTH Nav2 and SLAM**. They also omit post-commit work: the `guide_mate_perception` C++ port, `combined_node`/`guide_mate_bringup`, and `slam_fused.yaml`.

3. **On-disk clutter dominates the working tree.** A **5.4 MB vendored AWS SDK clone** (`aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/`, 2.9 MB is its own nested `.git` — gitignored, not a submodule); **loose AWS credentials + 2 expired tunnel tokens at repo root**; and inconsistent run-helper handling (`rviz/*.sh` tracked, `start.sh` gitignored). Worse: **`start.sh` actively re-clones the SDK at runtime** (lines 18–21) and `pip install`s the local copy (line 26) — so deleting the in-tree clone without fixing the script regenerates the clutter on the next run.

4. **A split commit breaks fresh builds.** `setup.py` (tracked) already declares `guide_mate_bringup = guide_mate_explorer.combined_node:main` (line 33), but `combined_node.py` is **untracked** — a fresh `clone + colcon build` produces a broken entry point. The `guide_mate_perception/` C++ package is also fully untracked. This is a build-correctness issue to resolve **before** any restructure lands.

**No credential was ever committed — git history is clean.** This is hygiene, not exposure remediation.

---

## Proposed structure (new repo top-level + `docs/`)

├── CLAUDE.md                       # KEEP authoritative; refresh: scan_fused, 2 pkgs, slam_fused.yaml, combined runner

├── .gitignore                      # UPDATE: add /secrets/; KEEP cred globs as defense-in-depth; drop /aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/ and start.sh lines
├── docs/
│   ├── README.md                   # KEEP docs index + security policy; fix secrets.local→secrets/; relink camera.md & power.md
│   ├── camera.md                   # MERGE  camera/README.md (thin) → camera/oak-d-camera-test.md → ONE file
│   ├── power.md                    # MERGE  power/README.md (thin)  → power/power-saving.md      → ONE file
│   ├── mapping/                    # KEEP subdir (3 substantial files — earns it)
│   │   ├── README.md               # UPDATE: scan_fused single source; add C++ port + combined-runner rows; advance status
│   │   ├── depth-perception.md     # UPDATE: add "C++ port (guide_mate_perception)" subsection; mark fusion hw-validated
│   │   └── rviz-visualization.md   # KEEP (verified accurate); fix one path ref → scripts/launch_rviz.sh
│   ├── network/                    # KEEP subdir as-is (3 substantial, verified-accurate files)
│   │   ├── README.md
│   │   ├── nuwave-connection.md
│   │   └── ros2-over-nuwave.md
│   └── aws-iot/                    # KEEP subdir as-is (3 content files + index — justified)
│       ├── README.md               # KEEP (sole home of robot-468 IoT setup-state table); add awsiotsdk==1.30.0 pin
│       ├── iot-core-overview.md
│       ├── service-integration.md
│       └── secure-tunneling.md
├── scripts/                        # NEW: group loose run-helpers
│   ├── launch_rviz.sh              # MOVE from rviz/ (tracked)
│   └── start.sh.example            # NEW sanitized template (env-driven paths; no in-tree SDK clone; pip awsiotsdk)
├── rviz/
│   └── turtlebot468_mapping.rviz   # KEEP (tracked RViz config — data, not a script)
└── src/                            # colcon src — TWO packages now
    ├── guide_mate_explorer/        # Python pkg — commit combined_node.py + combined.launch.py + setup.py line together
    └── guide_mate_perception/      # C++ fusion port — commit as a complete unit (CMakeLists/package.xml/src/launch)

── gitignored, NOT in tree ──────────────────────────────────────────────

Rewrite `nav2_glass.yaml` row + stack diagram to **`scan_fused` single fused source**; fix "How to run" (`autonomous_mapping` self-starts camera+fusion+SLAM+Nav2; `depth_perception` step is vestigial); advance status (scan_fused wired + fusion hw-validated, not pending); remove the unimplemented `/tmp/explore.log` tail; add rows for combined runner + C++ fusion node. |

Add **"C++ port (`guide_mate_perception`)"** subsection (motivation: Pi-4 CPU, ~10× cheaper; numerical-equivalence notes: float32/`lrint`/median/tie-break; `depth_lidar_fusion_cpp.launch.py`; run Python OR C++, same node name). Mark fusion **hardware-validated** (205 seen / 195 injected), note `scan_fused` is now the single costmap source, pointcloud→`oakd/scan` is legacy. |

| `docs/network/README.md` | **keep** | — | Non-derivable infra (fast-discovery-server invocation, `/etc/turtlebot4` env block, `192.168.186.0/24` layout, chrony chain, stale-IP cleanup). Optional one-liner: ASUS_98 fallback. |
| `docs/network/nuwave-connection.md` | **keep** | — | Irreplaceable 5 GHz regdom root-cause + exact `nmcli` WPA2-Enterprise profile + 120 s auto-revert safety. Verified current. |
| `docs/network/ros2-over-nuwave.md` | **keep** | — | Only home of the four-root-cause discovery-server debugging trail (DATABASE corruption, super-client TTY check, `hz --no-daemon`). Verified. |
| `docs/aws-iot/README.md` | **keep** (1 add) | — | Sole home of the robot-468 IoT setup-state table (Thing name, `sdk/test/*` policy scope, tunnel-opened-but-localproxy-missing) + redaction policy. Add `awsiotsdk==1.30.0` pin + relocated-SDK note when the in-tree clone is removed. |
| `docs/aws-iot/iot-core-overview.md` | **keep** | — | Conceptual anchor; irreplaceable file-to-IoT-concept mapping table + policy-scope note. |
| `docs/aws-iot/service-integration.md` | **keep** | — | Only in-depth outbound-integration + EC2-access doc. Optional one-line: flag `turtlebot468/cmd\|telemetry` as illustrative (current policy is `sdk/test/*` only). |
| `docs/aws-iot/secure-tunneling.md` | **keep** | — | Canonical tunnel how-to + honest blocked-status (localproxy not installed, policy lacks notify perms). |
| `rviz/launch_rviz.sh` | **move** | `scripts/launch_rviz.sh` | Run-helper; group with other helpers. Update the path ref in `rviz-visualization.md` and any hardcoded relative path inside the script. |
| `rviz/turtlebot468_mapping.rviz` | **keep** | — | Tracked RViz config (data, not a script). Stays under `rviz/`. |
| `src/.../combined_node.py` | **commit (bring under VCS)** | — | Untracked while `setup.py` already declares its entry point → breaks fresh clone+build. Commit with `combined.launch.py` + the `setup.py` line as one unit. |
| `src/guide_mate_perception/` | **commit (complete unit)** | — | Untracked C++ rclcpp port (CMakeLists.txt, package.xml, `src/depth_lidar_fusion_node.cpp`, `launch/depth_lidar_fusion_cpp.launch.py`). Commit as a whole package or leave out entirely — do not half-land. |

---

## On-disk cleanup (non-git clutter)

**Confirmed on disk:** `aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/` (5.4 MB clone), 4 credential files + `connect_device_package.zip` + `root-CA.crt` at root, 2 `c9109663-*AccessToken.txt`, and `start.sh` that re-clones + local-pip-installs the SDK.

1. **Remove the vendored SDK — AND stop the script from regenerating it.** Delete `aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/` (5.4 MB; 2.9 MB nested `.git` confuses grep/IDE indexers). **This is not enough on its own:** `start.sh` lines 18–21 re-`git clone --recursive` it into the workspace cwd and line 26 `pip install`s the local copy. The sanitized `scripts/start.sh.example` **must remove the clone-and-local-pip-install block** and instead depend on a pip-installed `awsiotsdk==1.30.0` (PyPI name). Pin it in `docs/aws-iot/README.md` (install command) and/or a `requirements.txt`. If a local checkout is ever needed for the samples, put it **outside the workspace** (`~/vendor/aws-iot-sdk/`) — never a foreign `.git` inside the colcon tree.

2. **Consolidate all secrets into a single gitignored `secrets/` dir.** Move `Turtlebot-468.private.key`, `Turtlebot-468.cert.pem`, `Turtlebot-468.public.key`, `Turtlebot-468-Policy`, `root-CA.crt`, `connect_device_package.zip` there. Ignore the **whole directory** (`/secrets/`). Point the bring-up script at the new path via env (`$IOT_CERT`, `$IOT_KEY`, `$IOT_CA`). History is clean — hygiene, not remediation.

3. **Delete the two expired tunnel tokens** `c9109663-destinationAccessToken.txt` and `c9109663-sourceAccessToken.txt` — single-use/short-lived, never usable (localproxy was never installed), now stale junk.

4. **`.gitignore` reconciliation (3 edits):**
   - **Add** `/secrets/` (one whole-dir line — closes the rename-bypass gap, e.g. `cert.pem.old`, `backup.key.bak`).
   - **Keep** the filename-shaped globs (`*.pem`, `*.key`, `*.crt`, `*-Policy`, `*AccessToken*`, `connect_device_package.zip`) as **defense-in-depth** — a conscious decision, not half-redundancy.
   - **Drop** the now-stale `/aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/` line (clone relocated out of tree) and the `start.sh` line (script moves to `scripts/start.sh` or `scripts/start.sh.example`).

5. **Resolve `start.sh` handling.** Currently gitignored (line 23) and invisible to collaborators, yet it's the documented bring-up convenience. Sanitize it (env-driven cert/endpoint paths reading from `secrets/`; drop the SDK clone block) and track it as **`scripts/start.sh.example`**; a real machine-specific `scripts/start.sh` can stay gitignored locally. Either way it moves under `scripts/` so root stops mixing README/CLAUDE with creds, the SDK clone, and a loose script.

6. **One-pass OAK naming/size reconciliation (do as a discrete step, not opportunistically).** The **OAK-D vs OAK-D-LITE** name and the **depth-image-size** facts (`640×480` actual vs `1280×720` `camera_info` vs `640×400`) are inconsistent **repo-wide**. Fix them in a single sweep across **`CLAUDE.md`**, **`src/guide_mate_explorer/config/oakd_mapping.yaml`**, and **`docs/camera.md`** so the merge doesn't introduce a fresh inconsistency. The `camera_info` trap (depthai reports 1280×720 but the image is 640×480) stays documented as the *reason*.

7. **Verify the result.** After moves/removals the working tree drops from ~5.8 MB clutter to ~0.4 MB of real src+docs+rviz. Run `git status --ignored` to confirm all secrets are still ignored and root holds only `README` / `CLAUDE` / `.gitignore` / `docs` / `scripts` / `rviz` / `src`.

---

## CLAUDE.md sync (specific edits so it matches current code)

Verified against `setup.py` (entry points), `src/guide_mate_perception/` (real C++ files), and the config migration. Required edits:

1. **Two packages, not one.** The ASCII tree (and the line-8 prose "this repo holds … `src/guide_mate_explorer/`") must list **both** `guide_mate_explorer/` (Python) and `guide_mate_perception/` (C++ fusion port).
2. **`scan_fused`, single fused source.** Replace every "`oakd/scan` as a 2nd separate costmap obstacle source" framing with "**`scan_fused`** — depth folded into the lidar scan, consumed by **both** Nav2 costmaps **and** SLAM via `slam_fused.yaml`." Affects the Mapping-stack-architecture section, the `nav2_glass.yaml` config bullet, and the glass-handling layers list.
3. **Soften "lidar-only SLAM."** SLAM now consumes `scan_fused`, so glass enters the SLAM map. Reword the `slam_toolbox` bullet accordingly and add **`slam_fused.yaml`** to the Config section.
4. **Add the combined runner.** Document `guide_mate_bringup` → `combined_node:main` (entry point present in `setup.py` line 33) and `combined.launch.py` in the Nodes/Launch sections.
5. **Add the C++ fusion node.** Note `guide_mate_perception` is a drop-in rclcpp replacement (same node name, ~10× cheaper on Pi-4); reference `depth_lidar_fusion_cpp.launch.py`. Run Python **or** C++, not both.
6. **Expand the `docs/` tree comment** to enumerate all sections: `aws-iot/`, `camera.md`, `mapping/`, `network/`, `power.md` (reflecting the camera/power merge).
7. **Advance the roadmap.** Move "point `slam_toolbox scan_topic` + Nav2 at `scan_fused`" from **Next** to **Done**. **Keep** the "on-robot SLAM-map localization sanity check pending" caveat — do not imply more validation than exists.

---

## Decisions needed from you (approve before I execute)

1. **Untracked code — all-or-nothing, BEFORE the doc restructure.** Confirm I should **(a)** commit `combined_node.py` + `combined.launch.py` + the `setup.py` entry-point line together, **and (b)** commit `guide_mate_perception/` as a complete C++ package. Alternative: revert the dangling `setup.py guide_mate_bringup` line instead. *(Required either way — a tracked entry point pointing at an untracked module breaks fresh clone+build.)* **Which: commit both, or revert the line?**
2. **Collapse the two single-file doc subdirs** (`camera/`→`camera.md`, `power/`→`power.md`) while keeping `mapping/`, `network/`, `aws-iot/` as README-fronted subdirs. This intentionally runs **two doc conventions side by side** (single files for single-doc topics, index-fronted dirs for multi-doc topics). **OK?**
3. **`start.sh` disposition:** track a sanitized **`scripts/start.sh.example`** (env-driven, no in-tree SDK clone, depends on pip `awsiotsdk`) and keep any real local `start.sh` gitignored. **Approve sanitize-and-template?**
4. **Vendored SDK:** delete the in-tree clone, pin **`awsiotsdk==1.30.0`** in docs/requirements, and (optionally) relocate a local checkout to `~/vendor/aws-iot-sdk/`. **Approve?** *(Note: `docs/camera/` is owned `root:root` unlike its ubuntu-owned siblings — the merge may need a one-time `sudo chown` first. Flagging so the move doesn't stall.)*
5. **`.gitignore`:** add `/secrets/`, **keep** the credential globs as defense-in-depth, drop the obsolete SDK-clone + `start.sh` lines. **Approve all four changes?**

---

## Risks / what's preserved

**Preserved (irreplaceable — untouched in content):** all 8 CLAUDE.md gotchas + OAK bring-up incantation; every hardware datum (MXID, USB IDs, bandwidth math, −1.0 A/~14 W power, 205-seen/195-injected fusion numbers); all network root-causes (5 GHz regdom, WPA2-Enterprise profile, discovery-server debugging trail, `192.168.186.0/24` layout); the robot-468 IoT setup-state table; the RViz operator knowledge (Lite-vs-Standard URDF, benign-log list). The merges and moves relocate files and refresh stale framing — they do **not** drop content.

**Risks (and mitigations):**
- **start.sh regenerates clutter.** Deleting the in-tree SDK without rewriting `start.sh` (lines 18–21, 26) re-creates the 5.4 MB clone on next run. *Mitigation: the script rewrite is part of the cleanup, not optional.*
- **Path breakage from merges/moves.** Collapsing `camera/`+`power/` and moving `launch_rviz.sh` breaks cross-links in `docs/README.md`, `depth-perception.md` (→ `../camera/oak-d-camera-test.md`), and `rviz-visualization.md`. *Mitigation: grep for old paths after every move; fix the one `launch_rviz.sh` ref and any hardcoded relative path inside the script.*
- **Over-claiming validation.** Marking fusion hardware-validated and committing the new code could imply the on-robot localization check passed. *Mitigation: keep the "on-robot SLAM-map sanity check pending" caveat in CLAUDE.md and mapping docs.*
- **Naming/size piecemeal drift.** Fixing OAK-D/Lite and 640×480/1280×720/640×400 one file at a time invites new inconsistency. *Mitigation: do it as the single discrete one-pass sweep across CLAUDE.md + oakd_mapping.yaml + camera.md (cleanup §6).*
- **Convention asymmetry.** Single-file docs alongside index-fronted dirs diverges from the current uniform "every dir has a README" pattern. *Acceptable for single-doc topics; call it out in the PR so reviewers expecting symmetry understand the multi-file dirs intentionally keep their indexes.*
- **`root:root` ownership on `docs/camera/`** may cause edit/move friction. *Mitigation: one-time `sudo chown` before the merge.*",

So after the in-tree clone is deleted, the next ./start.sh run re-creates the 5.4MB clone (with its nested .git) right back in the repo. The onDiskCleanup gates removal on 'ensure awsiotsdk is pip-installed' but does not flag that start.sh actively regenerates the clutter. (2) Three authoritative docs (CLAUDE.md, README.md, docs/mapping/) have drifted from code: they still describe the old `oakd/scan` separate-costmap-source design when the active nav2_glass.yaml + slam_fused.yaml have migrated to `scan_fused` as the single fused source for both Nav2 and SLAM, and they omit the post-commit work (guide_mate_perception C++ port, combined_node/guide_mate_bringup, slam_fused.yaml). (3) On-disk clutter dominates the working tree: a 5.4MB vendored AWS SDK clone (2.9MB its own nested .git, not a submodule), loose AWS credentials + expired tunnel tokens scattered at repo root, and inconsistent run-helper handling (rviz/ scripts tracked, start.sh gitignored). content.text:
The audit is done and the critique came back **sound** (no dropped value, no overreach). Here's the consolidated picture — cross-checked against my own reading of the files.

## The headline

Your instinct ("so many docs and mds") is right, but the cause is mostly **not** your real docs. Of the ~37 markdown files, **~20 belong to a vendored third-party clone** (`aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/`, 5.4 MB incl. its own nested `.git`) that's already gitignored — it's on-disk noise, not repo content. Your actual tracked docs are just **17 files, and they're genuinely good** (every hardware finding, IP, and gotcha is accurate and worth keeping).

The real problems the audit found:

1. **Over-fragmentation** — 5 doc subdirs each fronted by a section-index README; two (`camera/`, `power/`) wrap a *single* file behind a 12–19 line near-empty index. "Quick facts" are triplicated across `CLAUDE.md` ↔ `docs/README.md` ↔ each section README.
2. **Docs drifted from code** — `CLAUDE.md`/`README`/`mapping` still describe the retired `oakd/scan` "separate 2nd costmap source" design; the live configs migrated to **`scan_fused` as the single fused source for both Nav2 and SLAM**. The new `guide_mate_perception` C++ port, `combined_node`, and `slam_fused.yaml` are undocumented.
3. **A build-breaking split** (this one's important) — `setup.py` already declares `guide_mate_bringup = ...combined_node:main`, but `combined_node.py` is **untracked**. A fresh `clone + colcon build` produces a broken entry point. The whole C++ package is untracked too.
4. **On-disk clutter** — the 5.4 MB SDK clone, loose AWS creds + 2 expired tunnel tokens at repo root, and `start.sh` *re-clones the SDK at runtime* (so deleting the clone without fixing the script regenerates it). Git history is clean — no secret was ever committed.

## Proposed target structure

```
cs7980-guide-mate/
├── CLAUDE.md          # refreshed: scan_fused, 2 packages, slam_fused.yaml, combined runner
├── README.md          # fixed inventory + links
├── .gitignore         # add /secrets/; drop stale SDK-clone + start.sh lines
├── docs/
│   ├── README.md      # index + security policy (de-duplicated)
│   ├── camera.md      # ← MERGE camera/README + oak-d-camera-test  (kill the subdir)
│   ├── power.md       # ← MERGE power/README + power-saving         (kill the subdir)
│   ├── mapping/       # KEEP (3 substantial files earn the index) — refresh to scan_fused
│   ├── network/       # KEEP as-is (3 strong files, verified accurate)
│   └── aws-iot/       # KEEP as-is (3 files + the robot-468 setup-state table)
├── scripts/           # NEW: launch_rviz.sh (moved) + start.sh.example (sanitized)
├── rviz/              # just the .rviz config now
└── src/
    ├── guide_mate_explorer/    # Python
    └── guide_mate_perception/  # C++ fusion port
secrets/               # NEW gitignored dir — all certs/keys/policy/tokens consolidated here
```

**Net effect:** working tree drops from ~5.8 MB of clutter to ~0.4 MB of real src+docs; 5 doc subdirs → 3 + 2 flat files; everything irreplaceable preserved (relocated, never dropped).

Before I execute, three decisions are genuinely yours:

content.text:
Got your answers:
- **New code → skip** — I won't touch, commit, or revert anything under `src/`. (Heads-up: that leaves the dangling `setup.py` → `combined_node` entry point breaking a fresh `colcon build`; I'll note it but not act on it.)
- **Scope → restructure + content sync** — I'll reorganize *and* freshen the drifted docs.
- The **on-disk cleanup** answer didn't come through, so I'll hold all destructive on-disk actions (SDK deletion, creds → `secrets/`, token deletion, `start.sh`) and confirm those separately — I won't delete/move files I didn't create without an explicit go-ahead.

Before I edit, let me verify the backbone claim of the content sync — that the live configs actually migrated to `scan_fused` — so the doc edits are grounded in fact, not the audit's say-so.
